ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'milk tea' class (FOODON_03301099); no Thai-specific milk tea subclass exists. Matched at the closest parent class level.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers tea-based beverages with milk; no Thai milk tea-specific code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for Thai milk tea found in published SNOMED CT release.

preparation_method: mixed with condensed milk and evaporated milk
main_ingredients: Thai tea leaves (Ceylon tea with spices and food colouring)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Hot Thai Milk Tea is approximately one cup (250 ml / 250 g), as typically served in Thai restaurants and hawker stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Hot Thai Milk Tea — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Hot Thai Milk Tea (cr_id G00010412). All per-100g values are taken from the HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is not separately reported by HPB for this entry and is estimated from the condensed milk and evaporated milk composition using USDA FoodData Central reference values for those ingredients.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2008 — sweetened milk tea / condensed milk tea analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Hot Thai Milk Tea exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated by analogy to sweetened condensed milk tea as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. A GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (≤55) per the University of Sydney GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). gi_category is therefore set to 'low'. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 8.95 × 2.5) / 100 = 12.3 per 250 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage (8.72 g sugar/100 g) — regular SSB intake independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-driven sodium retention and weight gain (AHA 2018).
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Hot Thai Milk Tea; sugar at 8.72 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold and regular consumption is independently associated with elevated blood pressure regardless of the low sodium content (20 mg/100 g).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar load (8.72 g/100 g; ~22 g per 250 ml serving) from condensed milk and added sugar with negligible dietary fibre (0.1 g/100 g) causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid Hot Thai Milk Tea in its standard form; a 250 ml serving delivers approximately 22 g of rapidly absorbed sugar with virtually no fibre buffer, producing a significant glycemic spike — request unsweetened Thai tea with evaporated milk only as a lower-sugar alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from condensed milk (~0.66 g/100 g) and added sugar (8.72 g/100 g) contribute to atherogenic lipid profiles and endothelial dysfunction with regular consumption.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Hot Thai Milk Tea due to its combined saturated fat and high sugar content; regular sugar-sweetened beverage intake is associated with elevated triglycerides and LDL cholesterol — choose unsweetened tea with low-fat milk to reduce cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar from condensed milk promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3s, polyphenols in material quantity, or fibre) are present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Hot Thai Milk Tea in its standard sweetened form as the high sugar load from condensed milk promotes systemic inflammation; unsweetened Thai tea retains tea polyphenols without the inflammatory sugar burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot Thai Milk Tea (ชาไทยร้อน, Cha Thai Ron) is a traditional Thai beverage made by brewing strong Thai tea leaves — typically a blend of Ceylon tea with star anise, tamarind, and orange food colouring — and combining the brew with sweetened condensed milk and evaporated milk, served hot. The drink is rich, creamy, and warmly spiced with a distinctive orange-amber colour and a sweet, milky flavour profile. It is widely available in Thai restaurants and hawker stalls across Singapore and Southeast Asia, commonly consumed at breakfast or teatime. Per 100 g, it provides 51 kcal, 1.58 g protein, 1.05 g fat, and 8.95 g carbohydrate of which 8.72 g is sugar, with 0.1 g dietary fibre and 20 mg sodium. A standard 250 ml serving delivers approximately 127.5 kcal and 22 g of sugar, classifying it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB). Clinically, the high sugar content from condensed milk raises concern for Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease with regular consumption. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low, upper boundary) by analogy to sweetened condensed milk tea per Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Patients managing blood glucose or blood pressure should request unsweetened Thai tea with evaporated milk only as a lower-risk alternative.
common_user_queries: How many calories in hot Thai milk tea?
Can diabetics drink Thai milk tea?
Thai tea with condensed milk nutrition
Is Thai milk tea high in sugar?
Thai milk tea GI glycemic index

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010412) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown are estimated from USDA FoodData Central reference values for condensed milk and evaporated milk components, which is a minor adaptation clearly documented.
notes: Saturated fat (0.66 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.28 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.04 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central reference values for sweetened condensed milk (USDA FDC ID 173685) and evaporated milk (USDA FDC ID 171278) proportional to the typical Thai milk tea recipe composition, as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this entry. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g based on the absence of hydrogenated fats in the standard recipe. Vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and water_g are set to -1 as these micronutrients are not reported by HPB for this entry and no approved secondary source provides reliable values for this specific preparation. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened condensed milk tea from Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for Hot Thai Milk Tea exists in the approved GI source catalogue. Per the University of Sydney GI classification scale, GI ≤55 = low, 56–69 = medium, ≥70 = high; a GI of 55 therefore falls in the 'low' category (upper boundary), corrected from the previous draft which incorrectly assigned 'medium'.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low': GI value of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category per the University of Sydney GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Previous draft incorrectly assigned 'medium' despite GI=55 being classified as low.
